About the author

[/et_pb_text][et_pb_image src="https://www.orbitalring.space/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/paul-g-de-jong-orbital-ring-300x300.jpg" title_text="paul-g-de-jong-orbital-ring" _builder_version="4.27.4" max_width_tablet="200px" max_width_phone="" max_width_last_edited="on|desktop" animation_direction="bottom" border_radii="on|300px|300px|300px|300px" always_center_on_mobile="off" global_colors_info="{}" theme_builder_area="post_content"][/et_pb_image][et_pb_button button_url="@ET-DC@eyJkeW5hbWljIjp0cnVlLCJjb250ZW50IjoicG9zdF9saW5rX3VybF9wYWdlIiwic2V0dGluZ3MiOnsicG9zdF9pZCI6IjIxNiJ9fQ==@" button_text="LEARN MORE" button_alignment="left" _builder_version="4.27.4" _dynamic_attributes="button_url" custom_button="on" button_text_size="16px" button_text_color="#000000" button_bg_color="rgba(255,255,255,0)" button_border_width="2px" button_border_radius="0px" button_font="Montserrat|700|||||||" button_use_icon="off" custom_margin="50px||||false|false" custom_padding="14px|40px|14px|40px|true|true" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}" button_text_color__hover_enabled="on" button_text_color__hover="#31d190" theme_builder_area="post_content"][/et_pb_button][/et_pb_column][et_pb_column type="1_2" _builder_version="4.16" custom_padding="84px|||" custom_padding_tablet="0px|||" custom_padding_phone="0px|||" custom_padding_last_edited="on|tablet" global_colors_info="{}" custom_padding__hover="|||" theme_builder_area="post_content"][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="14px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}" theme_builder_area="post_content"]

Paul de Jong is a speculative science and hard science fiction author, a lifelong inventor, explorer, and student, and a serial entrepreneur with a deep love for physics. Over the years, he has examined many perspectives of space travel and infrastructure, working out the details in his journals.

The seeds for this book series were planted more than ten years ago while he was developing some inventions related to space travel. Working out the details in his journals led to these stories, which interweave fiction with technical non-fiction. They have been largely inspired by Isaac Arthur’s YouTube channel: @isaacarthurSFIA, “Science & Futurism with Isaac Arthur”.

You can find him on a few social networks.

Ready to Build an Orbital Ring?

Orbital Ring Engineering is your best tool to learn about what’s possible now, and what will be attainable in the near future!

Also explore our blog to find a collection of articles, opportunities to explore the math behind the science of orbital rings, a coding forum for pursuing ways to get us into space faster and with more efficiency, and more.

About The Book

and Astronomy's Shocking Twist

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][et_pb_column type="1_2" _builder_version="4.16" custom_padding="|||" global_colors_info="{}" custom_padding__hover="|||"][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="14px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" text_line_height_tablet="1.8em" text_line_height_phone="1.7em" text_line_height_last_edited="off|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

Orbital Ring Engineering is a deep dive into the physics, material science, and engineering required to construct an orbital ring. It presents a set of assumptions and guides the reader through a discovery process, delving deeply into the various materials, physics, electrical engineering, orbital mechanics, and mathematics involved in this endeavor.

The book invites readers to think about the challenges and contribute to the innovations. What are the optimal numbers? Are my assumptions correct?

This website presents the design concepts highlighted in the book, as well as some findings. To learn more about the math and assumptions presented in the book Orbital Ring Engineering, keep exploring this website.

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][et_pb_row disabled_on="off|off|on" _builder_version="4.27.4" custom_padding="3px|||||" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_column type="4_4" _builder_version="4.27.4" custom_padding="|||0px|false|false" global_colors_info="{}" custom_padding__hover="|||"][et_pb_image src="https://www.orbitalring.space/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/author-icon-09-4.png" title_text="author-icon-09-4" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][/et_pb_image][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" header_font="||||||||" header_2_font="Montserrat|800||on|||||" header_2_text_color="#000000" header_2_font_size="4.2vw" header_2_line_height="1.2em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="16px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" text_line_height_tablet="1.8em" text_line_height_phone="1.7em" text_line_height_last_edited="off|phone" header_2_font_size_tablet="40px" header_2_font_size_phone="25px" header_2_font_size_last_edited="on|desktop" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

About The Book

and Astronomy's Shocking Twist

  Orbital Ring Engineering is a deep dive into the physics, material science, and engineering required to construct an orbital ring. It presents a set of assumptions and guides the reader through a discovery process, delving deeply into the various materials, physics, electrical engineering, orbital mechanics, and mathematics involved in this endeavor. The book invites readers to think about the challenges and contribute to the innovations. What are the optimal numbers? Are my assumptions correct? This website presents the design concepts highlighted in the book, as well as some findings. To learn more about the math and assumptions presented in the book Orbital Ring Engineering, keep exploring this website.[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][et_pb_row _builder_version="4.16" custom_padding="19px||5px|||" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_column type="4_4" _builder_version="4.16" custom_padding="|||" global_colors_info="{}" custom_padding__hover="|||"][et_pb_text quote_border_weight="0px" _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.8em" quote_font="|700|on||||||" quote_text_align="left" quote_text_color="#31D190" quote_font_size="24px" header_text_align="left" custom_margin="-24px|-30px|2px|||" custom_padding="|30px||" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="14px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" text_line_height_tablet="1.8em" text_line_height_phone="1.7em" text_line_height_last_edited="off|phone" border_color_all="#000000" border_width_left="8px" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

“Astronomy’s Shocking Twist” is a hard science fiction series that unfolds over 800 years. It opens with the current political climate and the race to establish a human presence on the Moon.

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][et_pb_row _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" custom_padding="3px|||||" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_column type="4_4" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="14px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

“Astronomy’s Shocking Twist” is a hard science fiction series that unfolds over 800 years. It opens with the current political climate and the race to establish a human presence on the Moon. Everything shifts dramatically in 2035 when Samantha Celeste Jones reveals a groundbreaking discovery through a press report, much to the chagrin of the United States government.

This ten-part series delves into technological innovations and their consequences, intertwining hard science fiction narratives with detailed speculative science companion books that examine the technologies in depth.

[/et_pb_text][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="16px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

These books offer a journey into humanity’s future within such a scenario. They begin by describing the technologies employed in the stories, and the first few books will elaborate on these technologies in great detail. The third book in the series will mark the beginning of the story timeline and will be the first science fiction book in the ten-part series.

The series is divided into two distinct tracks: a speculative science track and a hard science fiction track. The speculative science track will begin with the first book published, which describes the technologies required to build a mass transit system for launching into space: Orbital Ring Engineering.

The term “hard science fiction” has several interpretations, so here are the rules for this series. I will do my best to use existing technologies or reasonable extrapolations from known technologies. There will be strict adherence to physics, materials science, and biology to the best of my ability.

The main area where things will delve into the realm of speculative science fiction is in engineering. Engineers will face significant challenges in the stories. I make no claims that they will be able to accomplish the tasks at hand; however, the survival of the human race will depend on them!

Chapter 1

The Evolution into Space and the Orbital Ring

In a century, commuting to space may become as routine as taking a train. Unlike a space elevator, an orbital ring is a true mass transit system into outer space. It is a megastructure capable of transporting millions of passengers and thousands of tons of cargo into space every day. It is a safe and reliable system capable of launching cargo to Mars with very little propellant. An orbital ring would enable fast, reliable, and safe transportation around the inner solar system. As we shall see in this book, out of all the potential methods of escaping Earth’s gravity, an orbital ring is the fastest, most reliable, and highest- capacity space mass transit system imaginable, and as we shall see, it is also one of the most feasible.

The first three chapters provide an overview as well as some historical perspective. We start to explore the orbital ring in Chapter 4 by examining the anchor lines that attach the ring to Earth’s surface. Chapters 5 and 6 cover the levitation system and propulsion systems, respectively. In Chapter 7, we will discuss returning to Earth. We will conclude in Chapter 8 by discussing the economics and why one should be built. Each chapter can stand on its own, so you can read these chapters in any order you like.

What is an Orbital Ring

Imagine the International Space Station (ISS). It’s a tin can that seems to !oat 400 km (250 miles) above the surface of the Earth. Now, imagine a rope running next to the ISS. If that rope went all the way around the planet and connected itself to form a solid ring, it would also remain in that orbit. If the rope were made to go faster, it would want to push outward toward a higher orbit. That force could then be used to support a geostationary platform.

This is, of course, an overly simplified description. We will explore the math in more detail in Chapter 5, but for now, you can imagine that although an individual particle on its own would follow the path of inertia, the fact that each section of the cable is restrained by the cable translates that inertia into the rope wanting to expand outward. The force pulling inward is, of course, the centripetal force of gravity. The excess cable velocity would create an outward centrifugal force.

%91Image 1.1 The orbital ring is not to scale. In reality, it would not be visible from this position in space or from the ground, with the possible exception of occasional reflections when the Sun is at just the right angle.%93

How to Build an Orbital Ring

The idea is simple: put a cable in orbit around the planet. Then encase that cable with an outer shell. Next, spin the inner cable faster while slowing the outer shell down. Once the outer shell is stationary relative to the ground, drop down some anchoring lines, two at a time, with one line positioned north of the cable and one south. Doing this at regular intervals will help keep the orbital ring in a stable location while the forces created by the motion of the inner cable work to counter the influence of gravity.

Some of those lines connecting to the ground can be strategically placed so they can be used as trolley car cables to move up and down from the cable platform. If the orbital ring anchor lines are conductors, they can transport any excess electricity generated by solar panels on the orbital ring to the surface. Mass drivers built on top of the orbital ring would be able to launch cargo and passengers into space at velocities well beyond Earth’s escape velocity. This is an orbital ring, in a nutshell.

%91Image 1.2: Centripetal vs. Centrifugal Force. According to Newton’s first law, a mass wants to follow its path of inertia. The centripetal force is the restraining force pulling the mass inward. The centrifugal force pulls outward against the centripetal force. Without the centrifugal force, the mass would fall straight inward following the direction of the centripetal force. If the centripetal force is removed, instead of following the direction of the centrifugal force, the mass would follow the path of inertia. This is why a centrifugal force is sometimes referred to as an imaginary force.%93

Building an orbital ring would be no small feat, but the great thing is that it is what one would call an engineering problem, not a physics problem. In this book, we will go into great detail about the physics, materials science, and engineering involved in building such a megastructure.

A Brief Historical Diversion

China pioneered rocket technology during the Song dynasty, which spanned from 960 to 1279 A.D.; however, it wasn’t until 1942 that rockets finally reached the threshold of space. The development of the V-2 rockets during World War II marked a significant milestone. Regrettably, the history of rocket technology is marred by violence.

In 1903, visionary Soviet rocket scientist Konstantin Tsiolkovsky formulated the rocket equation, laying the groundwork for modern rocketry. He further advanced the !eld by proposing the concept of multistage rockets in 1929. The Soviet Union successfully launched its inaugural rocket in 1928, marking a significant achievement in space exploration. After World War II ended, the dawn of the space race drastically accelerated progress in space.

The first animals in space were a group of fruit flies, launched to an altitude of 67 km at the tip of a V-2 rocket on February 20, 19471. These early experiments were designed to study the effects of cosmic rays on living organisms. A monkey named Albert II was blasted to an altitude of 133 km in a V-2 rocket on June 14, 1949, surviving the light but dying on impact.

A pair of Russian dogs, Tsygan and Dezik, were launched to an altitude of 100 km on July 22, 1951, by the Soviet Union. These were the first vertebrates to survive a trip to the Kármán Line, the internationally agreed-upon border with outer space.

The first manmade satellite to orbit the Earth was called Sputnik 1. It was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957, and its radio pings were heard around the world. This event marked the start of the Space Race.

%911. NASA. (n.d.). Animals in space. NASA History Division. https://history.nasa.gov/animals.html
2. NASA. (n.d.). Animals in space. NASA History Division. https://history.nasa.gov/animals.html%93

%91END OF CHAPTER 1 PREVIEW%93

header_2_font_size="30px" background_layout="dark" text_font_size_tablet="18px" text_font_size_phone="17px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|desktop" text_line_height_tablet="1.8em" The book is divided into three sections.

Section 1 of Orbital Ring Engineering serves as an introduction and provides a historical overview. It examines the various technologies that led to the concept of an orbital ring and offers an overview of what an orbital ring is, along with other related concepts. Section 2 of Orbital Ring Engineering begins by laying the groundwork for what is needed to build an orbital ring. It explores the details of where the initial ring should be placed, the material science involved, and a potential design. In this section, we examine the technology and delve deeply into the technical aspects required to make the orbital ring functional, making it the most technical of the sections. Section 3 of the book looks more closely at what an orbital ring can be used for and how it will be utilized. In section 3 of Orbital Ring Engineering we explore the reasons why we would build an orbital ring, and give some political and economic context.[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column_inner][/et_pb_row_inner][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_section][et_pb_section fb_built="1" admin_label="Testimonials" _builder_version="4.27.4" custom_padding="25px||25px||true|" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_row column_structure="3_5,2_5" use_custom_gutter="on" _builder_version="4.27.4" background_enable_color="off" width="100%" width_tablet="94%" width_phone="" width_last_edited="on|desktop" max_width="100%" module_alignment="left" custom_padding="70px||0px|||" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_column type="3_5" _builder_version="4.27.4" background_color="#000000" custom_padding="|||" global_colors_info="{}" custom_padding__hover="|||"][et_pb_image src="https://www.orbitalring.space/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/author-icon-12.png" align="center" _builder_version="4.27.4" width="50px" custom_margin="30px||||false|false" animation_direction="bottom" always_center_on_mobile="off" global_colors_info="{}"][/et_pb_image][et_pb_slider _builder_version="4.27.4" header_level="h4" header_font="Montserrat|700|||||||" header_font_size="26px" header_line_height="1.6em" body_font="|700|||||||" body_text_color="#31d190" background_color="rgba(255,255,255,0)" custom_padding="19px||73px|||" header_font_size_tablet="20px" header_font_size_phone="18px" header_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" global_module="634" saved_tabs="all" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_slide heading="The Orbital Ring is potentially the most transformative technology for helping humanity journey to space, and Paul provides a long ended deep dive of the capabilities and practical engineering challenges of this awesome megastructure." _builder_version="4.27.4" custom_css_slide_title="margin-bottom: 20px;" global_colors_info="{}" sticky_transition="on"]

About the author

[/et_pb_text][et_pb_image src="https://www.orbitalring.space/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/orbital-ring-engineering-paul-de-jong-book-author-300x300.jpg" title_text="orbital-ring-engineering-paul-de-jong-book-author" _builder_version="4.27.4" max_width_tablet="200px" max_width_phone="" max_width_last_edited="on|desktop" animation_direction="bottom" border_radii="on|300px|300px|300px|300px" always_center_on_mobile="off" global_colors_info="{}"][/et_pb_image][et_pb_button button_url="@ET-DC@eyJkeW5hbWljIjp0cnVlLCJjb250ZW50IjoicG9zdF9saW5rX3VybF9wYWdlIiwic2V0dGluZ3MiOnsicG9zdF9pZCI6IjIxNiJ9fQ==@" button_text="LEARN MORE" button_alignment="left" _builder_version="4.27.4" _dynamic_attributes="button_url" custom_button="on" button_text_size="16px" button_text_color="#000000" button_bg_color="rgba(255,255,255,0)" button_border_width="2px" button_border_radius="0px" button_font="Montserrat|700|||||||" button_use_icon="off" custom_margin="50px||||false|false" custom_padding="14px|40px|14px|40px|true|true" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}" button_text_color__hover_enabled="on" button_text_color__hover="#31d190"][/et_pb_button][/et_pb_column][et_pb_column type="1_2" _builder_version="4.16" custom_padding="84px|||" custom_padding_tablet="0px|||" custom_padding_phone="0px|||" custom_padding_last_edited="on|tablet" global_colors_info="{}" custom_padding__hover="|||"][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="16px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

Paul de Jong is a speculative science and hard science fiction author, a lifelong inventor, explorer, and student, and a serial entrepreneur with a deep love for physics. Over the years, he has examined many perspectives of space travel and infrastructure, working out the details in his journals.

The seeds for this book series were planted more than ten years ago while he was developing some inventions related to space travel. Working out the details in his journals led to these stories, which interweave fiction with technical non-fiction. They have been largely inspired by Isaac Arthur’s YouTube channel: @isaacarthurSFIA, “Science & Futurism with Isaac Arthur”.

You can find him on a few social networks.

A book, a Vision, and a Coding Community

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][et_pb_column type="2_5" _builder_version="4.16" custom_padding="70px|||" custom_padding_tablet="0px|||" custom_padding_phone="0px|||" custom_padding_last_edited="on|tablet" global_colors_info="{}" custom_padding__hover="|||"][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="16px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

While writing Orbital Ring Engineering, I started with a set of assumptions that turned out to be completely wrong. Some of the issues I encountered could be solved analytically, but the time-dependent processes, like orbital ring deployment, really needed iterative solutions.

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][et_pb_row _builder_version="4.16" custom_margin="|auto|43px|auto||" custom_padding="2px|||||" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_column type="4_4" _builder_version="4.16" custom_padding="|||" global_colors_info="{}" custom_padding__hover="|||"][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="16px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

That process led to some very surprising results, which I describe in Chapter 6 of the book. I did the numerical analysis using simple Python code, running it repeatedly to hone in on results that actually worked. The code was crude but magical at the same time. This got me thinking: why not create an open source project to optimize various space-based applications like orbital rings, space elevators, and mass driver systems?

Learn more about the project, and join our Python coding community.

item.excerpt:encoded: Learn how to build a planetary orbital ring, the future for space explorations. A hard science fiction book by visionary author Paul de Jong.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value:
This is an example page. It's different from a blog post because it will stay in one place and will show up in your site navigation (in most themes). Most people start with an About page that introduces them to potential site visitors. It might say something like this:

Hi there! I'm a bike messenger by day, aspiring actor by night, and this is my website. I live in Los Angeles, have a great dog named Jack, and I like piña coladas. (And gettin' caught in the rain.)

...or something like this:

The XYZ Doohickey Company was founded in 1971, and has been providing quality doohickeys to the public ever since. Located in Gotham City, XYZ employs over 2,000 people and does all kinds of awesome things for the Gotham community.

As a new WordPress user, you should go to your dashboard to delete this page and create new pages for your content. Have fun!
Orbital Ring Engineering | A Book by Paul de Jong
Learn how to build a planetary orbital ring, the future for space explorations. A hard science fiction book by visionary author Paul de Jong.
Orbital Ring Engineering | A Book by Paul de Jong
Learn how to build a planetary orbital ring, the future for space explorations. A hard science fiction book by visionary author Paul de Jong.
Orbital Ring Engineering | A Book by Paul de Jong
Learn how to build a planetary orbital ring, the future for space explorations. A hard science fiction book by visionary author Paul de Jong.

Our website uses cookie technology to offer a more complete browsing experience.

All the content of our website is protected in terms of copyright, according to US and international copyright laws.

global_colors_info="{}"]

Privacy POLICY

[/et_pb_text][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="16px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

At OrbitalRing.Space (the website) we respect your privacy and we will never share our patients’ data to third parties.

All personal data acquired by the website are subject to treatment based on principles of correctness, lawfulness, transparency and protection of users’ privacy, in accordance with US and international Data Protection Regulations.

This website does not collect personal data directly, but uses cookies to allow our users’ browsers to navigate our content more easily (see Cookie policy below).

Traffic on this website is measured using the free web analytics platforms offered by Google, such as Google Analytics, Google Search Console and Google Site. The performance of the site is measured in order to evaluate the effectiveness of our communication and the interest of users in our content. These analysis are performed for anonymous statistical aggregates and no direct association is made between individual users and website content.

For any communication, information, suggestion or complaint regarding privacy, please contact us.

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][et_pb_row module_id="cookie" _builder_version="4.27.4" custom_margin="|auto|50px|auto|false|false" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_column type="4_4" _builder_version="4.16" custom_padding="|||" global_colors_info="{}" custom_padding__hover="|||"][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" header_font="||||||||" header_2_font="Montserrat|800||on|||||" header_2_text_color="#000000" header_2_font_size="4.2vw" header_2_line_height="1.2em" module_alignment="right" header_2_font_size_tablet="40px" header_2_font_size_phone="25px" header_2_font_size_last_edited="on|desktop" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

COOKIE POLICY

[/et_pb_text][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="16px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

Our website uses cookie technology to offer our users a more complete browsing experience.

OrbitalRing.Space adopts cookie technology to make navigation simple and efficient for our users. Users who browse and interact with our website will see a minimum amount of information entered on the devices in use, i.e. computers and mobile devices, in small text files called “cookies”, saved in the directories used by the user’s browser.

Disabling cookies is an open option for all users, but without cookies some of the services on the website may not work properly and some pages will not be displayed correctly.

TYPES OF COOKIES

There are various types of cookies, some to make site navigation easier and more effective, others to enable certain features. The cookies used on our website allow to:

• memorize the preferences entered by users during the interaction with the site;
• facilitate the sharing of site content on social networks by users;
• analyze the use of the services and content provided by the site to optimize the browsing experience and services offered to the user.

TECHNICAL COOKIES

This type of cookie is strictly necessary for the proper functioning of some sections of the website. There are two categories of cookies: persistent cookies and session cookies.

Persistent cookies: once the browser is closed they are not deleted but remain active until the preset expiration date.

Session cookies: are deleted every time the browser is closed. These cookies, always sent from our domain, are necessary to allow the user to view and navigate the site correctly and will always be used and sent, unless users change the settings in their browser (thus interrupting the display of pages of the site).

THIRD PARTY ANALYSIS COOKIES

These cookies are used in order to collect information on the use of the site by users anonymously, and in particular: pages visited, time spent, traffic origin, geographical origin, age, gender and interests for the purpose of advertising campaigns. marketing. These cookies are sent from third-party domains external to the site, in our case from the Google Analytics platform.

COOKIES FOR THE INTEGRATION OF THIRD PARTY PRODUCTS AND SOFTWARE

This type of cookie integrates features developed by third parties and used within the pages of the webite, such as the characters used for texts, icons, preferences expressed in social networks to share site content or for the use of software services of third parties, such as social media content republished on this website. These cookies are sent from third party domains that offer their functionality to help us create better interaction performance for the pages of the site.

PROFILING COOKIES

These cookies are necessary to create user profiles in order to send advertising messages in line with the preferences expressed by the user within the pages of the site. Our website does not use profiling cookies.

CONSENT FOR TECHNICAL COOKIES

According to current legislation, OrbitalRing.Space is not required to seek consent for technical cookies, as they are necessary to provide the requested services. For all other types of cookies, consent can be expressed by the user with one or more of the following methods:

• through specific browser configurations or related computer programs used to navigate the pages of the website;
• changing the settings in the use of third-party services.

Both of these solutions could prevent the user from using or viewing parts of the site or from viewing and navigating it correctly.

THIRD PARTY COOKIES USED BY THE SITE

Our website may contain links to other websites that have their own privacy policy which may be different from the one adopted by our website. orbitalRing.Space is not responsible for the standard adopted by these sites. Our website integrates a series of services offered by leading companies in the market of web applications and services, necessary for the correct display, navigation, interaction and use of the site by web users.

The third-party services that use cookies, integrated into our website are:

* GPDR Cookie Consent Plugin, by Webtoffee User data is processed by the WordPress plug-in created by the Webtoffee company and adopted as part of the technological infrastructure by the webite, for the sole purpose of managing the user’s consent and preferences on the use of cookies. Collected data: user’s preference on the use of cookies on this site. Policy: Webtoffee Privacy Policy.

* Akismet, by Automattic The Akismet service is necessary to protect the pages of the site from spam and is provided by Automattic, the company that also owns the WordPress blog, the content management platform adopted by this site. Policy: Automattic Privacy Policy. Place of processing: USA

* Google Font, by Google Google Fonts is a service that allows you to incorporate and display custom font styles, managed by Google. Collected data: cookies and usage data on this site. Policy: Google Cookie Policy. Place of processing: USA

* Google Analytics, by Google The accesses and interactions of users on our site are analyzed through the Google Analytics service, managed by Google. Collected data: Cookies and site content sharing. Policy: Google Services Cookie Policy. Place of processing: USA

Privacy / Cookies on social media platforms

Please note that when you share any content via any share button you find on the web, including on the Studio Site, you are sharing your contact information and browsing / content preferences with such social media platforms. You can control which of your personal data is managed by the platforms by logging in and changing your permissions on your personal user profile page for each platform.

How to configure the browser to disable cookies

Follow Google’s instruction to disable cookies from your browser. It is also possible to install on your browser an extension provided by Google to disable Google Analytics’ cookies. This will affect your browsing and user experience not only on our website, but also generally while browsing other sites that implement the web analytics solution provided by Google.

VISIBILITY OF THIS PAGE

This page is visible through the link at the bottom of all pages of the website, following the simplified procedures for the information and the acquisition of consent for the use of cookies, as required by the latest EU General Data Protection Regulation (GPDR 2018).

For any communication, information, suggestion or complaint regarding privacy, please contact us.

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][et_pb_row module_id="copyright" _builder_version="4.27.4" custom_margin="|auto|50px|auto|false|false" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_column type="4_4" _builder_version="4.16" custom_padding="|||" global_colors_info="{}" custom_padding__hover="|||"][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" header_font="||||||||" header_2_font="Montserrat|800||on|||||" header_2_text_color="#000000" header_2_font_size="4.2vw" header_2_line_height="1.2em" module_alignment="right" header_2_font_size_tablet="40px" header_2_font_size_phone="25px" header_2_font_size_last_edited="on|desktop" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

COPYRIGHT AND INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY

[/et_pb_text][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="16px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

All the content of our website is protected in terms of copyright, according to US and international copyright laws.

All the content of the website is owned by OrbitalRing.Space and its author Paul de Jong and is protected in terms of intellectual property and copyright, unless explicitly attributed to other rights holders.

The photographic images posted on the website are largely our property, or have been acquired from websites offering royalty-free photographic images, or purchased and licensed for internet publishing. The quotes of our testimonials come from friends, followers and supporters of the project, who have given us full consent to use their testimonials to promote the website and the book.

We have integrated in blog articles some images from Google Images, found by searching for copyright-free photos (royalty free). We have processed these images, bringing them to the most appropriate size for our site, sometimes through small interventions to make them more readable. In the case of images found on sites managed with a Creative Commons license, as in the case of WikiMedia, we have reported the attribution credits as required by the license, adding a link to the original page of the image, with the Creative Commons license notes.

If you believe that one of the photos we use is covered by intellectual property, please notify us immediately by writing an e-mail. It will be our duty to integrate credits and copyright notices for their respective authors and rights holders, or to remove the image immediately, if requested by the copyright owner.

Any logos and registered trademarks published on these pages are the property of their respective owners. These trademarks are mentioned for informational purposes only, and the website has no rights over them. OrbitalRing.Space is in no way responsible for the content and services of websites that can be accessed through links, banners or articles published on this site. Furthermore, the existence of links to another site does not imply our approval or acceptance of responsibility for the content or use of such websites.

OrbitalRing.Space assumes no responsibility for any errors or omissions contained in this site or for any malfunctions or inaccuracies in the services offered by the website, and reserves the right to make changes to the content published on the site without any prior notice. OrbitalRing.Space cannot under any circumstances be held responsible for any partial or total damage, direct or indirect, deriving from the use of this website or other websites connected to it via links, as well as for any damage caused, directly or indirectly, from the indications on their web pages.

For any communication, information, suggestion or complaint relating to copyright and intellectual property on this site, please contact us.

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][/et_pb_section]
item.excerpt:encoded: Welcome to our privacy, cookie and copyright page. Learn about our privacy, cookie and copyright / intellectual property policies.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value:
Who we are

Suggested text: Our website address is: https://www.orbitalring.space.

Comments

Suggested text: When visitors leave comments on the site we collect the data shown in the comments form, and also the visitor’s IP address and browser user agent string to help spam detection.

An anonymized string created from your email address (also called a hash) may be provided to the Gravatar service to see if you are using it. The Gravatar service privacy policy is available here: https://automattic.com/privacy/. After approval of your comment, your profile picture is visible to the public in the context of your comment.

Media

Suggested text: If you upload images to the website, you should avoid uploading images with embedded location data (EXIF GPS) included. Visitors to the website can download and extract any location data from images on the website.

Cookies

Suggested text: If you leave a comment on our site you may opt-in to saving your name, email address and website in cookies. These are for your convenience so that you do not have to fill in your details again when you leave another comment. These cookies will last for one year.

If you visit our login page, we will set a temporary cookie to determine if your browser accepts cookies. This cookie contains no personal data and is discarded when you close your browser.

When you log in, we will also set up several cookies to save your login information and your screen display choices. Login cookies last for two days, and screen options cookies last for a year. If you select "Remember Me", your login will persist for two weeks. If you log out of your account, the login cookies will be removed.

If you edit or publish an article, an additional cookie will be saved in your browser. This cookie includes no personal data and simply indicates the post ID of the article you just edited. It expires after 1 day.

Embedded content from other websites

Suggested text: Articles on this site may include embedded content (e.g. videos, images, articles, etc.). Embedded content from other websites behaves in the exact same way as if the visitor has visited the other website.

These websites may collect data about you, use cookies, embed additional third-party tracking, and monitor your interaction with that embedded content, including tracking your interaction with the embedded content if you have an account and are logged in to that website.

Who we share your data with

Suggested text: If you request a password reset, your IP address will be included in the reset email.

How long we retain your data

Suggested text: If you leave a comment, the comment and its metadata are retained indefinitely. This is so we can recognize and approve any follow-up comments automatically instead of holding them in a moderation queue.

For users that register on our website (if any), we also store the personal information they provide in their user profile. All users can see, edit, or delete their personal information at any time (except they cannot change their username). Website administrators can also see and edit that information.

What rights you have over your data

Suggested text: If you have an account on this site, or have left comments, you can request to receive an exported file of the personal data we hold about you, including any data you have provided to us. You can also request that we erase any personal data we hold about you. This does not include any data we are obliged to keep for administrative, legal, or security purposes.

Where your data is sent

Suggested text: Visitor comments may be checked through an automated spam detection service.

item.title: About the Author and the Book
item.dc:creator: massimo
item.content:encoded:
[et_pb_section fb_built="1" admin_label="About" _builder_version="4.27.4" custom_margin="150px||||false|false" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_row column_structure="1_2,1_2" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_column type="1_2" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_image src="https://www.orbitalring.space/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/author-icon-09-4.png" _builder_version="4.27.4" max_width="400px" max_width_tablet="" max_width_phone="" max_width_last_edited="on|tablet" module_alignment="left" custom_margin="|||0px|false|false" custom_padding="|||0px|false|false" animation_direction="bottom" always_center_on_mobile="off" global_colors_info="{}"][/et_pb_image][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" header_font="||||||||" header_2_font="Montserrat|800||on|||||" header_2_text_color="#000000" header_2_font_size="4.2vw" header_2_line_height="1.2em" max_width_tablet="" max_width_phone="" max_width_last_edited="on|tablet" module_alignment="left" header_2_font_size_tablet="40px" header_2_font_size_phone="25px" header_2_font_size_last_edited="on|desktop" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

About the Book

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][et_pb_column type="1_2" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="16px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

Orbital Ring Engineering is a deep dive into the physics, material science, and engineering required to construct an orbital ring.

It presents a set of assumptions and guides the reader through a discovery process, delving deeply into the various materials, physics, electrical engineering, orbital mechanics, and mathematics involved in this endeavor.

The book invites readers to think about the challenges and contribute to the innovations. What are the optimal numbers? Are my assumptions correct?

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][et_pb_row column_structure="1_2,1_2" custom_padding_last_edited="on|tablet" _builder_version="4.27.4" custom_margin_tablet="0px||||false|false" custom_margin_phone="0px||||false|false" custom_margin_last_edited="on|tablet" custom_padding_tablet="9px||||false|false" custom_padding_phone="9px||||false|false" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_column type="1_2" _builder_version="4.16" custom_padding="|||" global_colors_info="{}" custom_padding__hover="|||"][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" link_font="|600|||||||" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="16px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

This website presents the design concepts highlighted in the book, as well as some findings. To explore the math, assumptions presented in the book Orbital Ring Engineering, keep exploring this website.

The seeds for this book series were planted more than ten years ago. Over the years, I have played with many of these ideas, working out details in my journals. The stories skirt the line between fiction and non-fiction and have been largely inspired by Isaac Arthur’s YouTube channel: @isaacarthurSFIA, “Science & Futurism with Isaac Arthur”

The genre is hard science fiction. My definition of hard science fiction is that all the laws of physics are obeyed as they are currently understood. I apply a relatively strict definition, limiting the text to technologies and materials that are either currently available or can reasonably be expected to become available in the near future. I also strive to keep the engineering within reasonably speculative bounds; however, the term “reasonable” is relative.

Reasonable becomes much more extreme when the motivation is extreme. Even if the economics present an extreme challenge, when the entire human race is at stake, the economics no longer really matter; what matters are the results. As the science fiction books will reveal, the motivation is indeed present, and humanity will do what it has to in order to make what needs to be done happen. Nothing else matters.

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][et_pb_column type="1_2" _builder_version="4.16" custom_padding="|||" global_colors_info="{}" custom_padding__hover="|||"][et_pb_image src="https://www.orbitalring.space/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/orbital-ring-engineering-cover-final1000.jpg" alt="Orbital Ring Engineering | A hard science fiction book by Paul de Jong" title_text="Orbital Ring Engineering | A hard science fiction book by Paul de Jong" force_fullwidth="on" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][/et_pb_image][et_pb_text quote_border_weight="0px" _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.8em" quote_font="|700|on||||||" quote_text_align="right" quote_text_color="#31d190" quote_font_size="24px" custom_margin="60px|-30px|60px||true|false" custom_padding="|30px||" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="14px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" text_line_height_tablet="1.8em" text_line_height_phone="1.7em" text_line_height_last_edited="off|phone" border_color_all="#000000" border_width_right="8px" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

As the science fiction books will reveal, the motivation is indeed present, and humanity will do what it has to in order to make what needs to be done happen. Nothing else matters.

About the author

[/et_pb_text][et_pb_image src="https://www.orbitalring.space/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/orbital-ring-engineering-paul-de-jong-book-author-300x300.jpg" title_text="orbital-ring-engineering-paul-de-jong-book-author" _builder_version="4.27.4" max_width_tablet="200px" max_width_phone="" max_width_last_edited="on|desktop" animation_direction="bottom" hover_enabled="0" border_radii="on|300px|300px|300px|300px" always_center_on_mobile="off" global_colors_info="{}" sticky_enabled="0"][/et_pb_image][/et_pb_column][et_pb_column type="1_2" _builder_version="4.16" custom_padding="84px|||" custom_padding_tablet="0px|||" custom_padding_phone="0px|||" custom_padding_last_edited="on|tablet" global_colors_info="{}" custom_padding__hover="|||"][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="16px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

Paul de Jong is a speculative science and hard science fiction author, a lifelong inventor, explorer, and student, and a serial entrepreneur with a deep love for physics. Over the years, he has examined many perspectives of space travel and infrastructure, working out the details in his journals.

You can find him on a few selected social networks.

item.excerpt:encoded: Orbital Ring Engineering is a deep dive into the physics, material science, and engineering required to construct a planetary orbital ring.

ORBITAL RING BLOG

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][et_pb_column type="1_2" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_text _builder_version="4.27.4" text_font="||||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.5em" text_font_size_tablet="16px" text_font_size_phone="16px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"]

Interested in going down the rabbit hole with many of the design concepts, findings, science and math assumptions highlighted in the book? Then this blog section is the right starting point for you to engage in some thought-provoking exploration of astronomy's shocking twists!

Enjoy the posts below, and don't forget to support the project by getting your own copy of the “Orbital Ring Engineering” book, available in hard cover, paperback, and Amazon Kindle formats.

item.title: Orbital Ring: An Open-Source Project for the Python Coding Community
item.dc:creator: Paul G. de Jong
item.content:encoded:
[et_pb_section fb_built="1" admin_label="Header Section" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" custom_padding="||9px|||" locked="off" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_row column_structure="2_5,3_5" use_custom_gutter="on" gutter_width="2" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" custom_margin="80px||||false|false" custom_padding="||6px|||" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_column type="2_5" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_post_title author="off" date="off" comments="off" featured_image="off" admin_label="Post Title - dynamic" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" title_font="Montserrat|800|||||||" title_font_size="30px" meta_font="|600||on|||||" meta_text_color="#31D190" global_colors_info="{}" title_text_color__hover_enabled="on|desktop" title_text_color__hover="#31D190" meta_text_color__hover_enabled="on|desktop" meta_text_color__hover="#000000"][/et_pb_post_title][et_pb_text admin_label="Article's Menu" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" link_font="Montserrat|500|||||||" link_text_color="#000000" link_font_size="15px" quote_font="Montserrat||||||||" quote_text_color="#000000" quote_font_size="15px" global_colors_info="{}" link_text_color__hover_enabled="on|desktop" link_text_color__hover="#31D190"]
I still have a dream An Open Source project Python Coding Community Hub on GitHub
[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][et_pb_column type="3_5" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_image src="@ET-DC@eyJkeW5hbWljIjp0cnVlLCJjb250ZW50IjoicG9zdF9mZWF0dXJlZF9pbWFnZSIsInNldHRpbmdzIjp7fX0=@" admin_label="Featured Image - dynamic" _builder_version="4.27.4" _dynamic_attributes="src" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][/et_pb_image][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][/et_pb_section][et_pb_section fb_built="1" admin_label="CONTENT Section" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" custom_padding="16px||6px|||" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_row module_id="dream" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" custom_margin="-6px|auto||auto||" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_column type="4_4" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_text admin_label="Full-widht Text" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" text_font="Montserrat|500|||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.3em" link_font="Montserrat|700|||||||" link_text_color="#31D190" text_font_size_tablet="17px" text_font_size_phone="17px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" header_2_font_size_tablet="" header_2_font_size_phone="26px" header_2_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" header_3_font_size_tablet="" header_3_font_size_phone="24px" header_3_font_size_last_edited="on|desktop" global_colors_info="{}" link_text_color__hover_enabled="on|desktop" link_text_color__hover="#000000"]

I still have a dream

While writing Orbital Ring Engineering, Vol. 1, I started with a set of assumptions that turned out to be completely wrong. Some of the issues I encountered could be solved analytically, but the time-dependent processes, like orbital ring deployment, really needed iterative solutions. That process led to some very surprising results, which I describe in Chapter 6 of the book. I did the numerical analysis using simple Python code, running it repeatedly to hone in on results that actually worked.[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][et_pb_row column_structure="1_2,1_2" module_id="open-source" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_column type="1_2" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_image src="https://www.orbitalring.space/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/orbital-ring-python-community-github2.png" title_text="orbital-ring-python-community-github2" align="center" force_fullwidth="on" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][/et_pb_image][/et_pb_column][et_pb_column type="1_2" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_text admin_label="Full-widht Text" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" text_font="Montserrat|500|||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.3em" link_font="Montserrat|700|||||||" link_text_color="#31D190" text_font_size_tablet="17px" text_font_size_phone="17px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" header_2_font_size_tablet="" header_2_font_size_phone="26px" header_2_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" header_3_font_size_tablet="" header_3_font_size_phone="24px" header_3_font_size_last_edited="on|desktop" global_colors_info="{}" link_text_color__hover_enabled="on|desktop" link_text_color__hover="#000000"]

An Open Source project on GitHub

The code was crude but magical at the same time. This got me thinking: why not create an open source project on GitHub to optimize various space-based applications like orbital rings, space elevators, and mass driver systems?

Eventually, I want the application to go beyond optimizing these specific space infrastructure projects, towards subjects like trajectories, such as a 4g launch from an orbital ring based on the ring’s specific parameters.

The goal is to build a Python interface that people can download and run on their own computers. The interface will show the equations involved and explain how they work.

[/et_pb_text][/et_pb_column][/et_pb_row][et_pb_row column_structure="1_2,1_2" module_id="community-hub" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_column type="1_2" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" global_colors_info="{}"][et_pb_text admin_label="Full-widht Text" _builder_version="4.27.4" _module_preset="default" text_font="Montserrat|500|||||||" text_font_size="18px" text_line_height="1.3em" link_font="Montserrat|700|||||||" link_text_color="#31D190" text_font_size_tablet="17px" text_font_size_phone="17px" text_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" header_2_font_size_tablet="" header_2_font_size_phone="26px" header_2_font_size_last_edited="on|phone" header_3_font_size_tablet="" header_3_font_size_phone="24px" header_3_font_size_last_edited="on|desktop" global_colors_info="{}" link_text_color__hover="#000000" link_text_color__hover_enabled="on|desktop"]

Join our Python Coding Community Hub on GitHub

People can improve the code, and if the community likes the improvements, they get added to the repository.

It’s both a design tool and a learning tool, meant to inspire curious minds who want a deep understanding of this technology and what it takes to build it.

Here is the link to our Orbital Ring Python coding community on GitHub, join us today!

[/et_pb_text][et_pb_button button_url="https://github.com/kjpaul/orbitalring" url_new_window="on" button_text="JOIN THE GITHUB COMMUNITY" button_alignment="center" _builder_version="4.27.4"
